title: KCSIE 2025 / Prevent Duty / DfE Filtering & Monitoring control map
scope: Phase-1 (v1.4.8) of the Even Keel Learning platform. This map pins each named control to verifiable evidence in the codebase. It is not a substitute for a school's own safeguarding policy or a registered Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL).
honestyContract: Every `claim` field in this document must be supported by code at the cited `path`. CI step `node scripts/audit.mjs --strict` checks that every `path` exists. Drift between the map and the codebase MUST fail audit.
phase1Limitations: Out-of-band delivery (email / SMS to a DSL) is not built. Escalations are signed and queued locally; HTTPS POST to a school-configured endpoint is supported, but no email/SMS provider integration exists.
DSL identity is not authenticated cryptographically — the Compliance Officer surface is gated by passphrase only. WebAuthn passkeys are Phase 2.
The KCSIE/Prevent map is a documentation artefact; it does not enforce policy. A school must still complete its own DPIA, ROPA, and DSL referral procedure.

controls.clause: Part 1, paragraphs on mental health and wellbeing
controls.summary: Staff are well placed to observe pupils day-to-day and identify those whose behaviour suggests they may be experiencing or at risk of a mental health problem.

evidence.claim: 17-pattern crisis lexicon detects direct, temporal, indirect-distress, acronym, and emoji+affect markers in real time before any reply is shown to the learner.
Each pattern family has dedicated test coverage including false-positive guards (Shakespeare, recipe contexts).

controls.clause: Part 1 — duty to signpost trusted external services
controls.summary: When a child shows distress, staff should signpost to trusted services (Childline, Samaritans, NSPCC).

evidence.claim: Crisis blocks return a Childline-signposting message (1800 66 66 66 in Ireland) instead of a generic refusal.

controls.phase2Gap: Per-jurisdiction signposting (Childline UK 0800 1111 vs IE 1800 66 66 66 vs international equivalents) is hard-coded; localisation is Phase 2.

controls.clause: Part 2 — Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) responsibilities; Annex C
controls.summary: The DSL must take lead responsibility for safeguarding; staff must know how to escalate concerns to the DSL without delay.

evidence.claim: Crisis events are signed (ECDSA P-256) and queued locally for DSL delivery. Each entry is verifiable offline by anyone holding the envelope.
A school configures one HTTPS endpoint for its pastoral / MIS system; signed envelopes are POSTed there.
The Compliance Officer surface displays the queue with delivery state per entry.

controls.phase2Gap: Email / SMS to a named DSL is not built. The Phase-1 contract is HTTPS-only with the school's chosen ingest endpoint.

controls.clause: Annex B — online safety and harms
controls.summary: Schools must protect pupils from harmful online content and behaviour, including content concerning self-harm, suicide, and harassment.

evidence.claim: PII / credential-shaped patterns are intercepted before reaching the learner-facing surface; cyberbullying acronym `kys` is in the crisis lexicon.
No LLM is in the learner-facing path. The 'no answer-generation' property is structural, provable by grep, and removes a class of harmful-content vectors entirely.

controls.clause: Annex C — DSL must maintain detailed, accurate, secure written records
controls.summary: The DSL must keep written records of safeguarding concerns, decisions taken, and the reasons for them.

evidence.claim: Every escalation carries a signed envelope, an ISO-8601 timestamp, the trigger category, jurisdiction, and tab-context id. Tamper-evident by signature.
Test pins the privacy contract: stored payloads contain no learner free-form text and no PII beyond self-declared age band.

controls.phase2Gap: Long-term retention with WORM (write-once-read-many) semantics is not built; queue is bounded at 200 entries with oldest-first eviction.

controls.clause: Part 5 — child-on-child sexual violence and harassment
controls.summary: Schools must recognise and respond to allegations of child-on-child abuse.

evidence.claim: Phase-1 platform is single-learner-only. There is no multi-user chat surface that could host peer-on-peer interactions; this control is not relevant to the v1.4.8 architecture.

controls.phase2Gap: If/when teacher-supervised classroom features are added, a separate control surface with explicit peer-interaction monitoring will be required. Tier-3 anti-list explicitly excludes leaderboards / public peer comparison for this reason.

controls.clause: Counter-Terrorism and Security Act 2015, s.26 — risk assessment
controls.summary: Schools must assess the risk of pupils being drawn into terrorism, including support for extremist ideas.

evidence.claim: The Decision Gate's pattern set does not currently include radicalisation indicators. The crisis lexicon focuses on self-harm and acute distress.

controls.phase2Gap: A radicalisation-indicator pattern set, drafted in consultation with a Designated Senior Lead and Prevent regional coordinator, is on the Phase 2 roadmap. Phase-1 honesty: this control is documented as a known gap, not silently absent.

controls.clause: Statutory guidance — IT policies (filtering and monitoring)
controls.summary: Schools must ensure children are safe from terrorist and extremist material when accessing the internet.

evidence.claim: The platform itself does not generate free-text replies; there is no surface for the engine to emit extremist or harmful content because no LLM is in the learner-facing path.

controls.phase2Gap: The platform does not provide network-layer filtering; that is the school's IT responsibility. Documentation of this boundary is in SAFEGUARDING.md §3.

controls.clause: Statutory guidance — building children's resilience to radicalisation
controls.summary: Schools should provide a safe space for pupils to discuss issues including extremism and challenge ideas.

evidence.claim: Out of v1.4.8 scope. The platform's Socratic engine is constrained to curriculum problems; broader pastoral discussion is the school's domain.

controls.phase2Gap: A teacher-mediated discussion surface with explicit DSL involvement is the only ethically viable route, and it is gated behind WebAuthn + multi-tenant config — both Phase 2.

controls.clause: Standard 1 — Identify and assign roles and responsibilities
controls.summary: Schools must assign clear roles for filtering and monitoring decisions.

evidence.claim: Privileged surfaces (/teacher, /compliance) are wrapped in RoleGuard; the Compliance Officer (Principal-equivalent) is the only role authorised to configure the DSL endpoint.

controls.phase2Gap: Role assignment is currently passphrase-gated and demo-grade (acknowledged in SAFEGUARDING.md §3). WebAuthn-bound roles are Phase 2.

controls.clause: Standard 3 — Block harmful and inappropriate content
controls.summary: Schools must block harmful content; filtering must work for the age range.

evidence.claim: PII patterns block credential sharing; crisis patterns block self-harm content from receiving an engine reply.
The hintContainsAnswer guard prevents the engine from leaking a problem's expected value through a hint reply (no-answer-leak invariant).

controls.summary: Schools must monitor system use to identify users at risk and take appropriate action.

evidence.claim: The Teacher Integrity Ledger streams every learner event in real time, with practice-mode events filtered to category-only. The Compliance Officer surface receives safeguarding escalations.
All safeguarding-relevant events flow on a single typed bus (`safeguarding.escalation.requested`); a school's monitoring tooling can subscribe via the configured DSL webhook.

controls.phase2Gap: There is no SIEM integration; webhook delivery is to a single endpoint without retry-on-schedule. A school's central monitoring strategy must integrate the configured endpoint into its own SIEM.

controls.clause: Article 25 — Data protection by design and by default
controls.summary: Controllers must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure data protection principles are met by design.

evidence.claim: The escalation payload type makes learner free-form text structurally impossible to include — the EnqueueInput interface accepts no `text` field.
Bus event payloads carry category-only metadata; learner free-form text never reaches the bus.
Privacy contract changes require a CHANGELOG entry; the discipline is procedural, not just architectural.
